turns.content: I'm trying to migrate some performance-critical game logic into our new Rust codebase, and I've hit a wall on the API surface. My current JSON wrapper uses serde_json::Value for dynamic data structures, but when I try to bump the minor version to add a new field, cargo-semver-checks flags it as a major breaking change even though it feels like an additive update. Can someone explain why this is happening?

The issue stems from how semver detects breaking changes in public APIs. When you add a field to a struct or modify a method signature, cargo-semver-checks interprets that as potentially breaking existing consumers who rely on the old structure. Think of it like adding a mandatory column to an Excel sheet; if your clients were writing formulas assuming certain columns existed and others didn't, suddenly changing what is required breaks their code even if you aren't removing anything. The tool checks for any change in public visibility or signature that could affect downstream users.

But wait, I only added a field and kept all existing fields optional, so how does that break things? My clients shouldn't care if I add more data. And also, why did my #[doc(hidden)] comment on an internal helper struct still trigger the failure?

You're right that adding fields alone isn't always breaking for well-designed APIs, but cargo-semver-checks runs strict checks because in Rust, even small signature changes can break serialization logic or deserialization patterns. As for the #[doc(hidden)] comment, while it hides items from generated docs, certain operations like macro expansion or trait implementations might still expose them to external inspection depending on visibility modifiers and where they are used. The tool scans all public APIs regardless of whether they have #[doc(hidden)], so if that struct is part of a larger public type's implementation details, it gets flagged.
I keep getting false positives from my semver check runs and I suspect #[doc(hidden)] is the culprit. Why does hiding a field in docs break compatibility even if it's not public?

That is a classic trap with cargo-semver-checks. When you mark something with #[doc(hidden)], the tool still counts it as part of your public API surface because semver considers any documented item, regardless of visibility attributes like #[cfg(test)] or #[doc(hidden)]. Think of it like a secret room in your house; even if no one walks through the door to enter it, the fact that you built the walls and floor means the room exists structurally. If someone adds documentation about that hidden field tomorrow, the tool sees a new public entry point and flags it as a breaking change. The solution is usually to exclude those items from the API report entirely by using #[doc(hidden)] combined with #[cfg(feature = "internal")] or simply avoid documenting them at all so they never appear in the generated report. This prevents the false positive while keeping your internal implementation details private.

I am following along with a blog post about detecting breaking changes in our library. I tried using the standard doc comments to mark items as hidden, but then ran into issues because some items were still showing up in the public API even though they should be invisible. Here is the code snippet from the post: pub mod internal { #[doc(hidden)] pub fn secret_calc() {} }

It sounds like you are hitting a common gotcha where #[doc(hidden)] does not automatically remove an item from the stable public API surface. Think of it like putting a sticky note on a door in your house; if someone is looking through the peephole, they can still see that there is something behind the door even if the note says 'do not disturb.' In Rust terms, #[doc(hidden)] only hides items from the documentation generated by `cargo doc` and IDEs, but it does not prevent them from being exported or included in binary size calculations. You need to actually remove the item from the module using `#[cfg(feature = "internal")]` or move the entire module into a private scope that is never re-exported through your crate's public API. The key concept here is distinguishing between visibility for humans (documentation) and visibility for machines (the compiled binary), because cargo-semver-checks cares about what actually reaches users, not just what appears in help text.

Ah, so the distinction is that documentation is for people reading manuals while the API surface is what gets shipped to customers? That makes sense. I'm confused though because my Cargo.toml has a feature flag set up, but cargo-semver-checks says there are breaking changes when I run it in CI even though I thought those items were already gated.

Exactly, and the feature gate alone isn't enough if that item is still reachable through re-exports. Imagine you have a warehouse where some boxes are locked behind a security door (the feature flag). If someone else can walk into your main office and grab those boxes without going through the security door, then from their perspective, they still have access to them. This happens when you do `pub use` on something inside a gated module; it acts like an open gate in your warehouse that bypasses the security check entirely. You need to ensure no public re-exports point to items that rely on internal feature flags unless you intend for those features to be part of the stable contract. This is why cargo-semver-checks runs in CI as a safety net: it compares the current API against previous versions to catch any item that slipped through the cracks and became visible unexpectedly.

That analogy helps visualize how public re-exports bypass feature gates. But I'm still stuck on running this check in our CI pipeline. How do we configure the build script so it compares against the previous version's API surface automatically? Can you show me a concrete example of how to set up cargo-semver-checks with proper flags?

To automate this detection, you need to configure your CI runner to execute `cargo semver-checks --package your-crate-name` against the previous release's data. Think of it like running a physical comparison between two sets of blueprints before signing off on construction; if any new walls or windows appear where there shouldn't be ones in the old design, you must stop immediately. In practice, you add a step to your CI workflow that fetches the last published crate version from crates.io, then runs semver-checks with flags like `--package` and optionally `--release-version` to compare against the specific tag. This ensures that any item you accidentally exposed through re-exports or removed without updating documentation will trigger a failure before your library ships.

Okay, so I need to fetch the previous version's data specifically? And if cargo-semver-checks fails because of those hidden items I couldn't catch earlier, what specific error messages should I look for in the output?

Yes, you must explicitly target the exact crate and version hash from your registry. When semver-checks detects breaking changes due to #[doc(hidden)] pitfalls or accidental re-exports, it will report specific categories like 'public items removed', 'items added without documentation', or 'traits changed'. The error output will list the exact path of the broken item, such as `internal::secret_calc`, showing precisely where your visibility logic failed. This granularity allows you to fix the code by removing the public re-export or applying proper conditional compilation flags before the next release.
